title: Microsoft 365 OAuth Token Abuse

sections.title: Microsoft 365 Alerts
items: Multiple successful logins from different countries within 15 minutes

sections.title: Additional Findings
items: OAuth token remains active after password reset

questions.question: What MOST likely occurred?
options: Patch failure on the mail server
questions.explanation: Multiple successful logins from different countries, OAuth consent, MFA being enabled, and token persistence after password reset strongly suggest OAuth token/session abuse after phishing.

questions.question: Why did password reset not fully solve the issue?
options: Password resets do not affect firewall rules
Mailbox forwarding rules automatically reset passwords
DNS cache entries preserve account access
OAuth tokens/app consent can remain valid after password changes
questions.explanation: Password reset alone may not revoke already-granted OAuth permissions or active tokens, so attacker access can persist.

questions.question: What should be revoked FIRST?
questions.explanation: The malicious OAuth consent/token is the active access path and should be revoked directly.

questions.question: What logs would help investigation most?
options: OAuth consent logs, sign-in logs, mailbox audit logs, and forwarding-rule logs
questions.explanation: OAuth consent, sign-in, mailbox audit, and forwarding-rule logs can show how access was granted, where it was used from, and what mailbox activity occurred.

questions.question: What controls could reduce similar attacks?
options: Admin consent workflow, OAuth app restrictions, phishing training, and UEBA/impossible-travel detection
questions.explanation: Restricting OAuth consent, requiring admin approval, training users, and detecting anomalous sign-ins reduce the chance and impact of OAuth phishing.

summary: Investigate OAuth consent abuse in Microsoft 365 where phishing leads to persistent mailbox access even after a password reset.

title: Memory-Resident Malware

sections.title: EDR Alerts
items: No malicious files found on disk

questions.question: What type of malware behavior is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: Encoded PowerShell, suspicious outbound traffic, no malicious files on disk, and disappearance after reboot indicate memory-resident or fileless malware behavior.

questions.question: Why might antivirus fail to detect it?
options: Antivirus cannot detect any PowerShell activity
The malware may avoid disk-based signatures by running mainly in memory
HTTPS traffic prevents all endpoint monitoring
questions.explanation: Fileless or memory-resident malware may evade traditional disk-based signature detection because there may be little or no malicious file to scan.

questions.question: What forensic evidence should be collected FIRST?
questions.explanation: Because the malware disappears after reboot and appears memory-resident, volatile memory should be collected before power loss or reboot.

questions.question: What containment action is MOST appropriate?
options: Isolate the affected host from the network while preserving evidence
Ignore because no file was found
questions.explanation: Isolation limits C2/exfiltration while preserving the system state for investigation.

questions.question: What indicators strongly suggest memory-resident malware?
options: High RAM usage, no disk artifacts, encoded PowerShell, suspicious outbound traffic, and disappearance after reboot
questions.explanation: The combination of volatile behavior, lack of disk artifacts, encoded PowerShell, and outbound traffic strongly supports memory-resident malware.

summary: Analyze fileless or memory-resident malware behavior where encoded PowerShell, outbound traffic, and volatile artifacts point to in-memory execution.

title: DNS Tunneling and Beaconing

sections.title: DNS Monitoring
items: Host WS-014 issued 2,900 DNS TXT queries in 45 minutes

sections.title: Network Evidence
items: No matching browser activity on WS-014
Outbound traffic is almost entirely UDP/53
Domain was registered less than 24 hours ago

questions.question: What activity is MOST likely occurring?
options: DNS tunneling or DNS-based data exfiltration
questions.explanation: High-volume TXT queries, Base64-like subdomains, a newly registered domain, and no browser activity strongly indicate DNS tunneling or DNS-based exfiltration.

questions.question: Which evidence most strongly supports exfiltration rather than normal DNS use?
options: The workstation is joined to the domain
The host has a valid DHCP lease
The DNS queries use long encoded-looking subdomains repeatedly
questions.explanation: Long encoded-looking subdomains repeated at high volume are unusual for normal DNS and commonly used to carry data in DNS tunneling.

questions.question: What should the analyst do FIRST to contain the threat?
options: Block the suspicious domain and isolate the affected host
Disable all internal DNS resolution permanently
questions.explanation: Blocking the domain limits the communication path, while isolating WS-014 prevents continued exfiltration or command-and-control activity.

questions.question: Which logs would help determine the internal source and scope?
options: DNS query logs, EDR telemetry, proxy logs, and DHCP mappings
questions.explanation: DNS logs show the queries, DHCP maps IP to host, EDR shows local process context, and proxy/log telemetry helps scope related outbound activity.

questions.question: Which SIEM detection would be MOST useful for this behavior?
options: Alert on any single DNS lookup
Alert only when DNS is disabled
Alert on high-frequency DNS TXT queries with long/high-entropy subdomains to rare domains
questions.explanation: A useful detection combines query type, frequency, entropy/length, and domain rarity to reduce false positives while catching tunneling behavior.

summary: Identify DNS tunneling and possible data exfiltration using high-volume TXT queries, encoded subdomains, and rare newly registered domains.

title: WMI and SMB Lateral Movement

sections.title: Authentication Logs
items: 4625 failed logon events against multiple admin accounts

sections.title: Endpoint and SMB Activity
items: svc_backup has no normal history of interactive access

questions.question: What attack behavior is MOST likely occurring?
options: Credential compromise followed by lateral movement
questions.explanation: A service account with privileged logon, WMI remote command execution, and admin share access indicates compromised credentials being used for lateral movement.

questions.question: Which MITRE ATT&CK techniques best match the observed activity?
options: T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service only
T1047 WMI and T1021.002 SMB/Windows Admin Shares
questions.explanation: The use of wmic maps to WMI execution, and access to c$ maps to SMB/Windows admin shares.

questions.question: What should be prioritized FIRST?
options: Disable or reset the compromised svc_backup account and isolate involved hosts
Ignore because it is an internal IP
questions.explanation: The privileged account may still be usable, so disabling/resetting it and isolating involved hosts directly limits attacker movement.

questions.question: Which evidence most strongly confirms lateral movement?
options: A single DNS query to an internal domain controller
WMI remote process creation and SMB admin share access
questions.explanation: Remote command execution using WMI and administrative share access are strong indicators of lateral movement.

questions.question: Which log sources would be MOST valuable next?
options: Windows Security logs, Sysmon, PowerShell/WMI logs, and EDR telemetry
questions.explanation: These sources can show logon type, process creation, WMI activity, account usage, and affected hosts.

summary: Triage suspected credential compromise and lateral movement involving a privileged service account, WMI remote execution, and SMB admin shares.

title: SQL Injection to Web Shell

sections.title: Server Evidence
items: cmd.exe /c whoami executed by web server process
Large outbound HTTPS transfer to a VPS provider

questions.question: What attack chain MOST likely occurred?
options: SQL injection followed by web shell upload and command execution
DNS poisoning followed by DHCP renewal
questions.explanation: The injection payload, successful responses, suspicious upload, command execution, and new admin account indicate SQL injection leading to web shell compromise.

questions.question: Which evidence indicates command execution through the web server?
options: cmd.exe /c whoami executed by the web server process
questions.explanation: The web server process launching operating system commands is direct evidence of server-side command execution.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests persistence?
options: The new local administrator account and retained web shell file
questions.explanation: A new admin account and uploaded web shell can provide continued access after the initial exploit.

questions.question: What should incident responders isolate FIRST?
options: The public website's CSS files only
questions.explanation: The compromised web server is actively executing commands and possibly exfiltrating data, so isolating it limits damage while preserving evidence.

questions.question: Which techniques are MOST relevant?
options: Exploit Public-Facing Application, Web Shell, Create Account, and Exfiltration Over Web Service
questions.explanation: The attack uses a vulnerable web app, web shell execution, account creation for persistence, and outbound transfer that may indicate exfiltration.

summary: Investigate a web application compromise chain involving SQL injection, web shell upload, command execution, persistence, and possible exfiltration.

title: Insider Data Exfiltration vs Account Compromise

sections.title: DLP Alerts
items: Files uploaded to personal cloud storage

sections.title: Context
items: Employee submitted resignation two days ago
Access originated from normal VPN location

questions.question: What are the TWO most likely explanations?
options: Insider data theft or compromised account activity
DNS cache poisoning or DHCP failure
questions.explanation: Large sensitive downloads, personal cloud upload, resignation, and anomalous timing support either insider threat or compromised account activity.

questions.question: What should analysts verify FIRST?
options: Whether the office lights were on
Whether the user had legitimate business justification for the access and upload
questions.explanation: Before assuming malicious insider behavior, analysts should verify identity, authorization, business need, and whether the activity was legitimate.

questions.question: Which evidence would strengthen insider threat suspicion?
options: Resignation, unusual access time, personal cloud upload, and no malware indicators
questions.explanation: The resignation, timing, and personal cloud upload indicate suspicious user-driven behavior rather than typical automated malware activity.

questions.question: Which containment action is MOST appropriate?
options: Temporarily suspend or restrict the account and block the upload destination while preserving evidence
questions.explanation: Restricting the account and upload path limits further exfiltration while preserving evidence for HR/legal/security review.

questions.question: How should severity generally be classified?
options: High, because sensitive data may have been exfiltrated
No incident, because the employee authenticated successfully
Low, because no malware was found
Informational, because the VPN country was normal
questions.explanation: Potential exfiltration of sensitive engineering data to personal cloud storage is high severity even without malware indicators.

summary: Distinguish insider data theft from account compromise using DLP, UEBA, resignation context, access patterns, and containment priorities.

title: Ransomware Containment and Recovery

sections.title: EDR Alerts
items: Suspicious process spawned from user Downloads folder

sections.title: Infrastructure Context
items: File server contains shared finance documents
Backups exist but restore status is untested
Several mapped network drives are visible from infected host

questions.question: What activity is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: File renaming, shadow copy deletion, and a suspicious executable are classic indicators of ransomware activity and recovery sabotage.

questions.question: What should be done FIRST?
options: Email all employees the ransom note
Isolate the infected host from the network
questions.explanation: Isolation prevents further encryption across mapped drives and network shares.

questions.question: Why is vssadmin.exe activity significant?
options: It may indicate deletion of shadow copies to prevent easy recovery
questions.explanation: Ransomware commonly deletes volume shadow copies to make recovery harder.

questions.question: What should be verified before restoration?
options: Backup integrity and that backups are not infected/encrypted
questions.explanation: Restoring from compromised or untested backups can reintroduce malware or fail during recovery.

questions.question: Which IR phase includes restoring clean systems from validated backups?
questions.explanation: Recovery focuses on safely restoring business operations after containment and eradication.

summary: Practice ransomware triage by identifying encryption behavior, shadow copy deletion, containment priorities, and safe recovery sequencing.

title: CVSS Policy-Based Prioritization

sections.title: Company Policy
items: Confidentiality impact is prioritized over availability impact
Public-facing systems are prioritized over internal systems when impact is similar
Exploitability and business criticality must be considered together

sections.title: Vulnerabilities
items: A: External portal, C:H/I:N/A:N, exploit available
B: Internal print server, C:N/I:N/A:H, exploit available
C: Internal wiki, C:L/I:L/A:N, no exploit
D: External marketing site, C:N/I:N/A:L, no exploit

questions.question: Which vulnerability should be remediated FIRST?
options: C: Internal wiki with low confidentiality and integrity impact
B: Internal print server with high availability impact
D: External marketing site with low availability impact
A: External portal with high confidentiality impact and exploit available
questions.explanation: Policy prioritizes confidentiality over availability, and the affected system is external with an available exploit.

questions.question: Why is the internal print server lower priority than the external portal?
options: The policy prioritizes confidentiality and public exposure over internal availability impact
Internal systems do not require patching
questions.explanation: The print server has high availability impact, but the external portal has high confidentiality impact and external exposure.

questions.question: Which factor most increases urgency for Vulnerability A?
options: The presence of a printer queue
questions.explanation: An exploit available against an internet-facing system with high confidentiality impact creates high remediation urgency.

questions.question: What should be considered if immediate patching is impossible?
options: Compensating controls such as WAF rules, access restrictions, or segmentation
questions.explanation: Compensating controls reduce risk when the primary mitigation, such as patching, cannot be applied immediately.

questions.question: What is the best analyst response to conflicting vulnerability priorities?
options: Apply organizational policy and business context to prioritize remediation
Only patch vulnerabilities with short names
questions.explanation: CySA+ vulnerability prioritization requires using policy, exposure, exploitability, and business impact rather than raw severity alone.

summary: Prioritize vulnerabilities using business policy, CVSS impact, exploitability, exposure, and compensating control reasoning.

title: Alert Fatigue and SOAR Automation

sections.title: SOC Metrics
items: Analysts manually close most phishing alerts
Several true positives missed during last month

sections.title: Recent Incident
items: Malware beaconed for 9 days before detection
SIEM generated alerts but analysts ignored them as duplicates

questions.question: What operational failure MOST likely occurred?
options: Alert fatigue caused analysts to miss true positives
questions.explanation: High alert volume, manual closure, missed positives, and burnout indicate alert fatigue and ineffective alert triage.

questions.question: Which improvement would MOST reduce repetitive manual work?
options: SOAR playbooks for duplicate alert handling and enrichment
Reducing log retention to one hour
questions.explanation: SOAR can automate enrichment, deduplication, ticketing, and standardized response steps for repeated alerts.

questions.question: Which SIEM change would improve detection quality?
options: Alert on every single event equally
Correlation tuning, baselining, deduplication, and severity adjustment
questions.explanation: Tuning and baselining reduce noise while preserving meaningful correlations that indicate real attacks.

questions.question: Which metrics should leadership monitor?
options: MTTD, MTTR, false positive rate, alert volume, and missed true positives
Only number of monitors per analyst
questions.explanation: Operational SOC metrics show whether detection and response quality is improving or degrading.

questions.question: What risk does alert fatigue create?
options: True compromises may be ignored or closed as duplicates
questions.explanation: When analysts are overloaded, they may miss or dismiss important alerts, increasing attacker dwell time.

summary: Assess SOC alert fatigue, duplicate alert handling, SOAR use cases, response metrics, and operational improvements.

title: Linux Privilege Escalation and Persistence

questions.question: What attack activity is MOST likely occurring?
options: Compromised account followed by privilege escalation, persistence, and possible exfiltration
questions.explanation: Successful login, sudo shell, SUID modification, cron job, outbound SSH, and archive creation show a compromise chain beyond normal administration.

questions.question: Why is chmod +s /bin/bash concerning?
options: It can create an SUID root shell privilege escalation path
questions.explanation: Setting the SUID bit on bash can allow users to execute a shell with elevated privileges, depending on system behavior and permissions.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests persistence?
options: Cron job under /etc/cron.d/ and SUID bash modification
questions.explanation: A cron job can re-execute attacker code, and SUID bash can provide a repeated privilege escalation mechanism.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests possible exfiltration?
options: Large archive in /tmp and new outbound SSH connections
questions.explanation: Attackers commonly archive data before transferring it out; outbound SSH connections provide a possible transfer channel.

questions.question: What should be contained FIRST?
options: The affected Linux host and the devops account/session
questions.explanation: Isolating the host and disabling/revoking the compromised account/session limits continued access, privilege escalation, and exfiltration.

summary: Investigate Linux compromise involving sudo abuse, SUID modification, cron persistence, archive staging, and possible exfiltration.

title: Impossible Travel Login
summary: Practice basic cloud identity triage by identifying impossible travel, useful logs, and immediate verification steps.

sections.title: Identity Alerts
items: User alice@company.com logged in from Lithuania at 09:05
Same user logged in from Singapore at 09:18
Both logins used the same browser user-agent

sections.title: Additional Context
items: MFA was successful on both logins
User does not have scheduled travel
No password reset has occurred yet

questions.question: What is the primary suspicious behavior?
questions.explanation: Two successful logins from distant countries within minutes is an impossible travel indicator.

questions.question: Which logs are MOST useful to review next?
options: Sign-in logs, MFA logs, and device/session details
questions.explanation: Cloud sign-in, MFA, device, and session logs help determine whether the login was legitimate or malicious.

questions.question: What should the analyst do FIRST?
options: Verify the activity with the user and review session details
questions.explanation: MFA success does not automatically mean the login was legitimate; verification and session review are appropriate first steps.

title: Conditional Access Bypass via Legacy Protocol
summary: Analyze cloud identity abuse where legacy authentication, successful mailbox access, and missing MFA enforcement suggest a conditional access gap.

sections.title: Mailbox Audit
items: Large number of messages accessed within 20 minutes

questions.question: What is the MOST likely issue?
options: Legacy authentication bypassed MFA/conditional access controls
questions.explanation: IMAP/legacy client access commonly lacks modern MFA enforcement and can bypass conditional access if not blocked.

questions.question: What should be disabled or restricted to reduce this attack path?
options: Legacy authentication protocols such as IMAP/POP where not required
questions.explanation: Blocking legacy authentication prevents attackers from using protocols that bypass stronger modern authentication controls.

questions.question: Which evidence supports business email compromise?
options: External forwarding rule and large mailbox access
A normal DNS lookup to the domain controller
questions.explanation: Mailbox access plus forwarding rules strongly indicate mailbox compromise or BEC activity.

questions.question: What should be done FIRST to contain active access?
options: Revoke sessions/tokens, disable legacy protocol access, and remove forwarding rules
questions.explanation: Revoking sessions and blocking the legacy access path directly interrupts the attacker's current access.

questions.question: Which policy improvement is BEST long term?
options: Conditional access requiring modern authentication and blocking legacy clients
questions.explanation: Modern auth enforcement and legacy protocol blocking reduce the chance of MFA bypass through old protocols.

title: Suspicious File Hash Identification
summary: Practice basic malware triage by using hashes, filename impersonation, and EDR context to identify a suspicious executable.

sections.title: Host Context
items: Legitimate Windows service host is usually svchost.exe
User recently downloaded a ZIP attachment
No approved software install was scheduled

questions.question: Which detail is MOST suspicious about the filename?
options: It uses svch0st.exe with a zero to impersonate svchost.exe
It is too short to run
It is stored on a Windows host
questions.explanation: svch0st.exe uses a zero to imitate the legitimate svchost.exe process name.

questions.question: Which technique helps identify whether the file is known malicious?
questions.explanation: A cryptographic hash can be compared against malware databases or internal threat intelligence.

questions.question: What should be done first if execution is confirmed malicious?
options: Isolate the host and preserve evidence
Ignore the alert because the name looks similar to Windows
questions.explanation: Isolation limits spread and preserves evidence for investigation.

title: DLL Side-Loading via Signed Binary
summary: Investigate suspicious execution where a trusted signed executable loads an unexpected DLL from a user-writable directory.

sections.title: EDR Process Tree
items: VendorUpdate.exe is digitally signed by a trusted vendor

sections.title: Network and File Evidence
items: Outbound HTTPS connection to rare domain update-cdn-check.net
No official vendor updater normally runs from C:\Users\Public

questions.question: What technique is MOST likely being used?
options: DLL side-loading using a trusted signed executable
questions.explanation: A signed executable loading an unexpected DLL from a user-writable path is a classic DLL side-loading pattern.

questions.question: Why does the signed executable not prove the activity is benign?
options: Digital signatures always fail on Windows
Signed files cannot connect to the network
Signed binaries can be abused to load malicious DLLs
questions.explanation: Attackers can abuse legitimate signed binaries to execute malicious DLLs and evade simple allow-listing.

questions.question: Which artifact should be analyzed first?
options: The unexpected version.dll loaded from C:\Users\Public
questions.explanation: The unusual DLL is likely the malicious payload and should be hashed, analyzed, and correlated with execution telemetry.

questions.question: Which detection would best catch similar behavior?
options: Any HTTPS traffic from any host
Signed process loading DLLs from user-writable directories
questions.explanation: Detecting DLL loads from user-writable paths by signed binaries can identify side-loading attempts while reducing noise.

questions.question: What is the BEST containment action?
options: Isolate the affected endpoint and collect the executable/DLL for analysis
Ignore because the parent file is signed
questions.explanation: Isolation prevents continued C2 or spread, while collecting both files preserves evidence.

title: Newly Registered Domain Beaconing
summary: Identify basic command-and-control beaconing using repeated outbound HTTPS requests to a newly registered domain.

sections.title: Proxy Logs
items: Host HR-LAPTOP03 requested https://cdn-update-service-check.com/api every 60 seconds
Requests are small and consistent in size

sections.title: Endpoint Context
items: No browser window open during the requests
User opened an email attachment earlier that morning

questions.question: What is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: Periodic small requests to a new domain from PowerShell strongly suggest beaconing.

questions.question: Which evidence is most suspicious?
options: Regular 60-second timing to a newly registered domain
The user has an email account
questions.explanation: Regular timing plus rare/new infrastructure is a strong C2 indicator.

questions.question: What should the analyst do first?
options: Isolate HR-LAPTOP03 and block the suspicious domain
questions.explanation: Containment should target the affected host and communication path.

title: Domain Generation Algorithm Pattern
summary: Triage suspected malware using many failed DNS lookups to random-looking domains followed by one successful connection.

sections.title: DNS Logs
items: Host WS-220 queried 400 random-looking domains in 10 minutes

sections.title: Network Evidence
items: One domain resolved successfully and was contacted over TCP/443
Process responsible: unknown executable in AppData
No similar pattern observed from other hosts

questions.question: What behavior is MOST likely?
questions.explanation: Many random-looking domains with many NXDOMAIN results are common in DGA-based malware trying to find active C2 domains.

questions.question: Why are many NXDOMAIN responses suspicious here?
options: DGA malware may try many generated domains until one resolves
Normal Windows patching requires hundreds of NXDOMAINs
NXDOMAIN always means DNS is disabled
questions.explanation: DGA malware often generates many candidate domains, most of which do not exist.

questions.question: Which host should be investigated first?
questions.explanation: WS-220 is the source of the suspicious DNS behavior and outbound connection.

questions.question: Which detection logic is best?
options: Alert on high NXDOMAIN volume plus random-looking domains from a single host
questions.explanation: Combining volume, entropy/randomness, and source host reduces false positives and catches DGA behavior.

title: RDP Logon from Unusual Workstation
summary: Practice identifying suspicious RDP activity using Windows logon type, source host context, and administrative account use.

sections.title: Context
items: admin.helpdesk normally logs in only from JUMPBOX01
Several failed logons occurred before success

questions.question: What does Logon Type 10 generally indicate?
options: Remote interactive logon such as RDP
questions.explanation: Windows Logon Type 10 commonly indicates a remote interactive session such as RDP.

questions.question: Why is this suspicious?
options: The account name contains a dot
Privileged RDP access came from an unusual workstation after failed attempts
The source is internal so it is automatically safe
questions.explanation: Deviation from admin access patterns plus failed attempts raises suspicion of credential compromise or lateral movement.

questions.question: Which action is most appropriate first?
options: Verify the session, isolate the source if malicious, and protect the admin account
questions.explanation: The analyst should validate the session and contain the source/account if unauthorized.

title: PsExec Remote Service Execution
summary: Investigate lateral movement using remote service creation, admin share access, and privileged logon events.

sections.title: Windows Logs
items: Event ID 4624 Logon Type 3 for admin01 from 10.0.7.90
Event ID 7045: Service PSEXESVC installed on HOST-22
Event ID 4688: cmd.exe spawned by services.exe

sections.title: Network Evidence
items: admin01 normally uses a jump server, not 10.0.7.90

questions.question: What activity is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: PSEXESVC service creation, ADMIN$ access, and cmd.exe spawned by services.exe are strong PsExec-style lateral movement indicators.

questions.question: Which evidence most strongly supports PsExec-like behavior?
options: PSEXESVC service creation and ADMIN$ access
questions.explanation: PsExec commonly creates a temporary service and uses SMB admin shares for remote execution.

questions.question: Which systems should be investigated?
questions.explanation: Both source and target are involved in the lateral movement path and should be investigated.

questions.question: What is the best immediate containment?
options: Disable/reset the admin01 account if unauthorized and isolate involved hosts
Ignore because SMB is allowed internally
questions.explanation: Protecting the privileged account and isolating affected hosts limits further movement.

title: Reflected XSS Attempt
summary: Identify a simple reflected cross-site scripting attempt from web server logs and choose appropriate validation and remediation steps.

sections.title: Web Access Logs
items: Source IP repeated similar payloads 18 times

sections.title: Application Context
items: Search term is reflected on the results page
No output encoding is currently configured

questions.question: What vulnerability is likely being tested?
questions.explanation: A script tag inside a reflected search parameter is a common reflected XSS test.

questions.question: What remediation is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Reflected XSS is commonly mitigated with proper output encoding, validation, and secure content handling.

questions.question: Why does HTTP 200 matter?
options: The server accepted and returned a response to the request
questions.explanation: A 200 response means the request was processed; further validation is needed to see if script execution is possible.

title: SSRF to Cloud Metadata Service
summary: Analyze a server-side request forgery attempt targeting cloud instance metadata and credentials.

sections.title: Cloud Context
items: Web app runs on cloud compute instance
Instance role has read access to object storage
Unusual object storage API calls occurred 3 minutes later

questions.question: What attack is MOST likely being attempted?
options: Server-side request forgery against cloud metadata service
questions.explanation: The 169.254.169.254 address is commonly associated with cloud instance metadata services and is a common SSRF target.

questions.question: Why is the metadata endpoint sensitive?
options: It hosts public CSS files only
It may expose temporary credentials for the instance role
questions.explanation: Cloud metadata endpoints may return temporary credentials tied to the instance role.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests impact beyond probing?
options: Unusual object storage API calls shortly after the metadata request
questions.explanation: Object storage calls shortly after the metadata access suggest credentials may have been obtained and used.

questions.question: Which mitigation is BEST?
options: Allow all URLs in fetch parameters
Block metadata access from the application where possible and enforce IMDS protections/least privilege
questions.explanation: SSRF risk is reduced through metadata protections, URL allow-listing, egress restrictions, and least-privilege roles.

title: Unusual Sensitive File Download
summary: Practice first-step insider threat triage for abnormal file downloads without immediately assuming guilt.

sections.title: Context
items: No malware indicators on the endpoint

questions.question: What should the analyst do FIRST?
options: Verify business justification and investigate the session
questions.explanation: The analyst should verify whether the access was legitimate before concluding malicious insider activity.

questions.question: Which tool or control is most relevant?
questions.explanation: DLP detects sensitive data movement, and UEBA helps determine whether behavior is unusual for the user.

questions.question: What evidence would increase concern?
options: Normal access during business hours with approved ticket
Upload to personal storage or use of an unapproved external destination
questions.explanation: External upload or unapproved destination increases the likelihood of exfiltration.

title: Privileged Insider Source Code Theft
summary: Triage suspected privileged insider theft involving source code cloning, personal Git remotes, and legal/HR-sensitive containment decisions.

sections.title: DLP and Git Logs
items: Senior developer cloned 44 private repositories in 35 minutes
Outbound push to personal Git provider blocked by DLP

questions.question: What is the MOST likely risk?
options: Privileged insider attempting source code exfiltration
questions.explanation: Mass cloning, personal remote creation, competitor context, and DLP block suggest possible insider exfiltration.

questions.question: Why is this not automatically malware-related?
options: Competitor job offers are network indicators
No malware indicators and activity used authorized developer tools
questions.explanation: The evidence points to user-driven activity using legitimate tools rather than malware execution.

questions.question: What should be done with the account?
options: Temporarily restrict repository access while preserving evidence and involving HR/legal as appropriate
Ignore because the user had access
questions.explanation: Restricting access limits further loss while preserving evidence and coordinating with appropriate teams.

questions.question: Which evidence would be most important to preserve?
options: Git audit logs, DLP events, repository access logs, and blocked upload details
questions.explanation: These records establish what was accessed, what was attempted, and whether data left the environment.

questions.question: How should severity be classified?
options: No incident because the user authenticated
Low because no malware was found
High because proprietary source code may be leaving the organization
questions.explanation: Potential theft of sensitive source code is high severity even if performed with valid credentials.

title: Single Workstation Ransomware Alert
summary: Practice basic ransomware response by identifying encryption behavior and choosing containment before recovery.

sections.title: Endpoint Alert
items: Suspicious executable running from Downloads folder

sections.title: Context
items: No file server encryption observed yet

questions.question: What is MOST likely happening?
questions.explanation: File renaming, ransom note creation, and suspicious execution strongly indicate ransomware.

questions.question: What should be done FIRST?
questions.explanation: Isolation prevents spread to mapped drives or network shares.

questions.question: Why should restoration not be first?
options: The active process may continue encrypting or reinfect restored files
questions.explanation: Containment and eradication should happen before safe recovery.

title: File Server Encryption Spread
summary: Triage ransomware spreading from a workstation to a shared file server through mapped drives.

sections.title: File Server Alerts
items: Thousands of file rename operations from user j.smith
Files renamed to .paynow across Finance share

sections.title: Endpoint Evidence
items: Backups exist but last restore test was 9 months ago

questions.question: Which host is the likely initial source?
questions.explanation: The file server activity originated from WS-77, which ran suspicious invoice_viewer.exe.

questions.question: What should be contained first?
options: WS-77 and active SMB sessions to FILE01
questions.explanation: The source workstation and its file server sessions are enabling encryption spread.

questions.question: Which evidence helps determine scope?
options: File server audit logs, EDR telemetry, SMB session logs, and backup status
questions.explanation: These logs identify affected files, source activity, active connections, and recovery readiness.

questions.question: What should be verified before restoring data?
options: Backup integrity and whether backups are clean
The color of the network cable
questions.explanation: Recovery depends on validated, clean backups and knowing the infection is contained.

title: False Positive Banner Detection
summary: Practice vulnerability validation when a scanner reports an outdated version that may be caused by banner mismatch.

sections.title: System Context
items: Reverse proxy sits in front of backend
No exploit attempts found in WAF logs

questions.question: What should the analyst do first?
options: Validate the actual exposed version manually
questions.explanation: A banner-based finding should be manually validated before remediation decisions.

questions.question: Why could this be a false positive?
options: The scanner may be reading a misleading banner or reverse proxy response
Critical CVSS findings are always fake
questions.explanation: Reverse proxies and stale banners can cause scanners to misidentify backend versions.

questions.question: What evidence would confirm the risk?
options: Actual exposed vulnerable version and reachable exploit path
questions.explanation: Risk is confirmed by proving the vulnerable component is actually exposed and exploitable.

title: Patch Exception for Legacy System
summary: Use compensating controls and risk acceptance logic for a legacy system that cannot be immediately patched.

sections.title: Vulnerability Finding
items: Legacy lab controller runs unsupported OS

sections.title: Business Context
items: System does not require internet access
Currently reachable from broad internal subnet

questions.question: What is the BEST mitigation if patching is unavailable?
options: Network segmentation and strict access control
questions.explanation: Segmentation and access control reduce exposure when patching cannot be done.

questions.question: What should be documented?
options: Risk acceptance or exception, compensating controls, owner, and review date
questions.explanation: Exceptions should be tracked with business ownership, controls, and review timelines.

questions.question: Which control would reduce exploitability most?
options: Allow access only from required management hosts
Allow access from every internal subnet
questions.explanation: Restricting network access to required hosts limits attacker reachability.

questions.question: How should the vulnerability be prioritized?
options: Ignored because the system is old
Based on exploitability, exposure, business impact, and compensating controls
questions.explanation: Vulnerability management uses exposure and business context, not CVSS alone.

title: Duplicate Alert Deduplication
summary: Practice basic SOC operations by identifying duplicate alert noise and choosing SOAR/deduplication improvements.

sections.title: SOC Queue
items: 600 alerts generated for the same phishing campaign
Same sender, same URL, same attachment hash

sections.title: Operational Context
items: Mean time to respond is increasing

questions.question: What is the main problem?
questions.explanation: Many alerts represent the same underlying campaign and create unnecessary manual work.

questions.question: Which improvement helps most?
questions.explanation: Grouping similar alerts and using SOAR playbooks reduces repetitive work.

questions.question: Which metric would show improvement?
options: Reduced mean time to respond and lower duplicate alert volume
questions.explanation: MTTR and duplicate alert volume directly measure response efficiency.

title: Detection Engineering Regression
summary: Investigate why a SIEM rule failed after a logging pipeline change and choose validation steps to restore detection quality.

sections.title: Detection Issue
items: Rule previously detected Office spawning PowerShell
After EDR upgrade, the rule stopped firing
Raw events still exist in the data lake

questions.question: What is the MOST likely cause of the detection failure?
options: Office can no longer spawn processes
Field mapping/schema change broke the SIEM rule
questions.explanation: The underlying logs still exist, but field names changed, so the rule no longer matches.

questions.question: What should be done first?
options: Update the detection logic to match the new schema and test against known sample events
questions.explanation: Detection logic must be aligned with the current schema and validated against known-good test cases.

questions.question: Which process would prevent similar issues?
options: Detection-as-code with regression tests and schema monitoring
questions.explanation: Detection engineering benefits from versioned rules, test events, CI checks, and schema-change monitoring.

questions.question: Which metric may reveal this failure in production?
options: Sudden drop to zero alerts for a historically active detection
questions.explanation: A sudden disappearance of expected detections can indicate pipeline or rule breakage.

questions.question: What is the biggest operational risk?
options: True attacks may bypass detection due to broken rules
questions.explanation: Broken detection rules create visibility gaps and allow attacks to go unnoticed.

title: SSH Brute Force Attempt
summary: Identify basic SSH brute force or username enumeration using Linux authentication logs.

sections.title: auth.log
items: Failed password for invalid user admin from 203.0.113.50
Failed password for invalid user test from 203.0.113.50
Failed password for invalid user oracle from 203.0.113.50

questions.question: What activity is MOST likely occurring?
options: SSH brute force or username enumeration
questions.explanation: Repeated failed SSH logins for invalid users from one source are typical of brute force or enumeration.

questions.question: What should be checked next?
options: Whether any successful logins occurred from the same source
questions.explanation: The key triage question is whether the attempts led to successful authentication.

questions.question: Which control could reduce this risk?
options: Allowing root login from the internet
MFA/keys, rate limiting, fail2ban, and restricting SSH exposure
questions.explanation: Strong authentication and rate limiting reduce SSH brute force risk.

title: Suspicious Cron Download Execution
summary: Analyze Linux cron persistence where a root job repeatedly downloads and executes a remote script.

questions.question: Why is this cron job suspicious?
options: It downloads and executes a remote script every minute as root
questions.explanation: A root cron job piping remote curl output into bash every minute is high-risk persistence/execution behavior.

questions.question: Which persistence mechanism is present?
questions.explanation: Cron provides scheduled execution on Linux and can be abused for persistence.

questions.question: Which detail is especially suspicious about the creator?
options: www-data created a root cron job
questions.explanation: A web service account creating a root cron job suggests web compromise or privilege abuse.

questions.question: What is the BEST containment action?
options: Isolate the host, preserve the cron file/script evidence, and block the remote server
questions.explanation: Containment should stop remote execution while preserving evidence for root cause analysis.
